did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed XAI-SkinCADx, a six-stage explainable deep ensemble framework for skin cancer diagnosis. It utilized hybrid feature extraction (GLCM, LBP), deep learning ensembles (DenseNet201 combined with VGG16, EfficientNetB5, and MobileNetV2), Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) for temporal patterns, and multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification. The model was trained on the ISIC skin cancer dataset to classify nine distinct classes of skin lesions, achieving 95.63% accuracy. Explainable AI methods (Grad-CAM++ and LIME) were also employed to visualize decision-making.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents XAI-SkinCADx, a novel six-stage explainable deep ensemble framework for skin cancer diagnosis. It describes original experimental work including hybrid feature extraction, deep learning ensemble construction (DenseNet201, VGG16, EfficientNetB5, MobileNetV2, BiLSTM, SVM), training on the ISIC dataset, and performance evaluation (95.63% accuracy). It includes an ablation study and results, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: XAI-SkinCADx: A Six-Stage Explainable Deep Ensemble Framework for Skin Cancer Diagnosis and Risk-Based Clinical Recommendations
brief_summary: This study introduces XAI-SkinCADx, a six-stage explainable deep ensemble framework for skin cancer diagnosis using dermoscopy images. The model was trained on the ISIC skin cancer dataset, which was augmented with 5,023 images to balance nine distinct lesion classes. The architecture combines hybrid feature extraction (GLCM, LBP), deep learning ensembles (DenseNet201 with VGG16, EfficientNetB5, and MobileNetV2), BiLSTM for temporal patterns, and a multiclass SVM for final classification. The framework achieved 95.63% accuracy, 96.2% precision, 95.7% recall, and an AUC of 0.97. Explainability was provided via Grad-CAM++ and LIME, with LIME demonstrating superior localization accuracy, while a clinical recommender system enabled risk-based triage.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the nine distinct classes in the ISIC dataset used for training and testing. Balanced to 444 training and 111 testing images after augmentation. AUC of 0.85 reported for the final ensemble model.

all_task_types.target: Nine distinct classes of skin lesions (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopy images into nine distinct diagnostic categories using a deep ensemble framework (DenseNet201, VGG16, EfficientNetB5, MobileNetV2, BiLSTM, and Multiclass SVM). The model predicts discrete lesion classes with an accuracy of 95.63%, precision of 96.2%, recall of 95.7%, and AUC of 0.97. Additionally, the system categorizes predictions into risk levels (low-risk, medium-risk, high-risk) for clinical triage.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Skin Lesion Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dataset of dermoscopy images categorized into nine distinct classes of skin lesions. The original dataset contained 2,367 images. For this study, the dataset was augmented with rotation, zoom, shift, and flip operations to balance the classes, resulting in a total of 7,390 images (5,023 new images added to the original 2,367).

use.training: Used for training the XAI-SkinCADx deep ensemble framework after augmentation and class balancing.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance with an 80:20 train-test split applied to the augmented dataset.
use.other: The original un-augmented subset (2,249 training, 118 testing) was initially described before augmentation, but the final model used the augmented split.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide statistical confirmation of reliability and eliminate the possibility that performance was due to overfitting or chance on a single split.
To report the average performance and variability across the 5-fold cross-validation folds for accuracy, F1-score, and AUC.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It used 5-fold cross-validation to provide statistical confirmation of reliability and reported mean and standard deviation for performance metrics to eliminate the possibility that performance was due to overfitting or chance on a single split.

architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet pre-trained models (DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, VGG16, EfficientNetB5) initialized with pre-trained layers up to the top layers.

stages.description: Hybrid feature extraction using GLCM-based dimensionality reduction and Local Binary Patterns (LBP) visualization.
Integrated CNN Ensembles: DV-25 (DenseNet201 + VGG16), DE-25 (DenseNet201 + EfficientNetB5), and DM-25 (DenseNet201 + MobileNetV2). DM-25 was selected as the primary feature extractor.
Temporal modeling using Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) on features from DM-25.
Final classification using Multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM).
Dermatology-focused LIME-based recommendation platform for risk-based triage.

training_details.environment: Google Colab Pro+ with NVIDIA A100 Tensor Core GPU

preprocessing_techniques: Bilateral filtering for denoising (diameter=9, sigmaColor=75, sigmaSpace=75)
Histogram equalization applied to the V channel of the HSV color space to improve contrast
Data augmentation including rotation (20 degrees), zoom (0.2), horizontal/vertical shift (0.15), and flipping
Conversion to grayscale for handcrafted feature extraction (LBP and GLCM)
Class balancing via augmentation to ensure equal distribution (444 training and 111 testing images per class)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal and vertical flipping (mirror swapping of axis y and axis x)
Dataset augmented to balance nine lesion classes, resulting in 444 training and 111 testing images per class (total 5,023 new images added to original 2,367)

conclusions: The XAI-SkinCADx six-stage explainable deep ensemble framework achieved high diagnostic performance on the augmented ISIC dataset, with 95.63% accuracy, 96.2% precision, 95.7% recall, 95.9% F1-score, and an AUC of 0.97.

The framework utilizes a hybrid approach combining GLCM/LBP feature extraction, a DM-25 ensemble (DenseNet201 + MobileNetV2), BiLSTM for temporal patterns, and a multiclass SVM for final classification.
LIME demonstrated superior localization accuracy compared to Grad-CAM++, achieving an IoU of 0.78 and Dice score of 0.87, whereas Grad-CAM++ achieved an IoU of 0.70 and Dice score of 0.81.
The model performed best for Vascular Lesion, Seborrhoeic Keratosis, and Dermatofibroma classes when evaluated with LIME interpretability, with Dice scores of 0.91, 0.89, and 0.87 respectively.
The system enables risk-based clinical triage by categorizing lesions into low-risk, medium-risk, and high-risk levels, facilitating timely dermatology consultation and reducing clinical burden.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalization, noting that the ISIC dataset may not fully represent the variability of real-world clinical conditions, such as differences in image-acquisition equipment.

The authors suggest that future improvements should include larger multi-center datasets, advanced generative augmentation (e.g., GANs), cost-sensitive learning, and ensemble methods to address class imbalance and overfitting.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC dataset, which may not fully represent real-world clinical variability, such as differences in image-acquisition equipment, lighting conditions, and skin tones across diverse populations.

Class balancing was achieved through synthetic data augmentation (rotation, zoom, shift, flip), which the authors acknowledge may not reproduce the rich variability of true-world dermatology cases and could lead to overfitting.

The model exhibited significantly lower performance for Melanoma and Pigmented Benign Keratosis (AUC of 0.68 for both), indicating potential difficulties in distinguishing these visually overlapping classes compared to others like Vascular Lesion (AUC 0.98).

The clinical recommender system is not integrated with Electronic Health Records (EHRs) or patient-clinical data, limiting its ability to provide personalized recommendations based on comprehensive patient history.
The framework requires high-end computational resources (NVIDIA A100 GPUs via Google Colab Pro+), which may limit its deployment and utility in resource-constrained clinical regions.
Explainability methods (Grad-CAM++ and LIME) are noted as not always definitive and may fail to align with expert clinician interpretations, particularly in ambiguous or complex conditions.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values); statistical reliability is established only through 5-fold cross-validation (Accuracy: 94.87% ± 0.42, AUC: 0.965 ± 0.006).

The dataset used was augmented from 2,367 to 7,390 images to balance nine classes, resulting in 444 training and 111 testing images per class, which differs from the natural class distribution of the original ISIC dataset.
